dashboard.title: 5-Week Angular
dashboard.description: 25 lessons across 5 weeks. Build real-world Angular applications with guided exercises, challenges, and Claude-powered assistance.

about.subtitle: A modern learning platform built around the ancient philosophy of organized, purposeful knowledge.
about.thothNamed: is named after Thoth, one of the most important deities in ancient Egyptian mythology.
about.thothWisdom: god of wisdom, knowledge, writing, and learning
about.thothAligns: This symbolism aligns closely with the purpose of this platform.
about.structuredDesc: Knowledge organized into a clear, progressive path — 5 weeks, 25 lessons.
about.deepDesc: Encouraging real comprehension over passive consumption — think, then build.
about.practiceDesc: Each lesson paired with hands-on challenges and guided solutions.
about.mission: "Thoth Academy is designed as a modern interpretation of this philosophy — a place where knowledge is organized, explored, and applied through hands-on experience."
about.learnMoreDesc: Read about the ancient Egyptian god of wisdom on Wikipedia.

guide.title: Angular Senior Certification
guide.subtitle: Claude project instructions to get the most out of each session — how to work together, session formats, and the full day-by-day schedule.

overview.heading: What This Course Is
overview.desc1: This is a structured 5-week intensive self-study course using Claude as your personal Angular instructor. You have a Claude Project set up with custom instructions that give Claude your full context — React background, timeline, target certification, and day-by-day schedule.
overview.desc2: Every session picks up exactly where you left off. No re-explaining. No lost context.

w1.theme: Angular core — components, DI, signals, change detection

profile.heading: My Profile
profile.backgroundValue: Seasoned React engineer — hooks, context, component patterns, React Router, react-hook-form, Redux/Zustand, React Testing Library, TypeScript, JS ecosystem

setup.heading: Setting Up Your Claude Project
setup.intro: A Claude Project stores your instructions and reference files persistently. Every conversation you start inside the project loads your context automatically — you never have to re-explain your background or timeline.

step1.title: Create the project
step1.description: In claude.ai, click Projects in the left sidebar → New project. Name it 'Angular Senior Cert Prep'. This is your permanent study space.

step2.title: Add project instructions
step2.description: Click the pencil/edit icon inside the project. Paste the content from angular-cert-project-instructions.md (the file generated alongside these PDFs). This tells Claude your background, schedule, and session format preferences.

step3.title: Upload reference PDFs
step3.description: Click 'Add content' or the upload icon in your project. Upload all PDF files from this course — one per day, per week. Claude can reference them when reviewing your code or when you ask 'what should I study today?'

step4.title: Always start sessions from inside the project
step4.description: Click 'New chat' WHILE you are inside the project view — not from the home sidebar. If you start from home, your project instructions are not loaded.

workingRules.heading: How to Work With Me — Always
workingRules.rule1: Draw React analogies when introducing Angular concepts
workingRules.rule2: Explicitly flag when a concept has NO React equivalent — spend extra time on these: DI, structural directives, change detection / Zone.js / OnPush / zoneless, RxJS Observables
workingRules.rule3: When I share code, review it as a senior Angular certification examiner would — flag poor patterns, not just bugs
workingRules.rule4: Point out React habits that would cost me marks (mutating arrays with OnPush, methods in templates, missing track, inject() outside injection context)
workingRules.rule5: Reference the day's PDF content when relevant

teaching.title: Teaching Session
teaching.step1: Concept explanation with React analogy (or NO EQUIVALENT flag)

quiz.title: Quiz Session
quiz.step1: Give all 10 MCQ (A/B/C/D) first — I answer all, then you score
quiz.step2: For every wrong answer: explain why, show correct code, give 1 follow-up question

coding.title: Coding Session
coding.trigger: "[Week X coding challenge] — here is my solution: [paste]"
coding.step1: Review as senior examiner: correctness, patterns, performance
coding.step2: What a senior would do differently
coding.step3: Point out any React habits that crept in

weakSpot.title: Weak-Spot Session
weakSpot.step1: Keep drilling with targeted questions until I get 3 consecutive correct
weakSpot.step2: Then give a micro coding exercise on that topic

mockExam.title: Mock Exam
mockExam.trigger: "Give me a full senior mock exam — 40 questions"
mockExam.step1: All 40 at once, I answer all before you score
mockExam.step2: Score by category, explain every wrong answer

routine.heading: Your Daily Study Routine
routine.intro: Following this routine every day keeps Claude calibrated to where you are and maximises the value of each session.

block1.title: Open a new chat inside the project and set the context
block1.step1: Start with: "I'm on Week X, Day Y — [topic]. Let's begin."
block1.step2: Claude loads your instructions and knows exactly where you are.

block2.title: Teaching or coding session on today's topic
block2.step2: For teaching days: ask Claude to teach, then do the mini-challenge.
block2.step3: For coding days: read the spec, set the timer, build it, paste for review.

block3.title: Quiz session or weak-spot drilling
block3.step1: End the session with: "Quiz me on today's topic — 10 questions."
block3.step2: Score yourself. Any topic where you score below 7/10 goes on your weak-spot list.

block4.title: Update your project instructions
block4.step1: Edit the 'Current Progress' section: update the week/day, check off mastered topics, add new weak spots.
block4.step2: This keeps Claude perfectly calibrated for tomorrow.

w1.title: Angular Core + Signals
w1.theme: Establish the Angular mental model from a React foundation

d1.name: Monday — Components & Modern Template Syntax
d1.p2: Template bindings: {{ }} interpolation, [prop] property, (event) event, [(ngModel)] two-way
d1.p3: Modern control flow: @if / @else if / @else, @for with mandatory track, @switch
d1.p4: @defer with all triggers: on idle, on viewport, on interaction, on hover, on timer(ms), when condition

d2.name: Tuesday — Dependency Injection
d2.p3: Injector tree: root → component → child — scope determines instance count
d2.p4: Provider recipes: useClass, useValue, useFactory, useExisting
d2.p5: InjectionToken<T> for non-class values (strings, config objects, primitives)

d3.name: Wednesday — Signals & Reactivity
d3.p1: signal<T>(init) — read with signal(), write with .set(), .update()
d3.p2: computed(() => expr) — lazy, memoised, auto-tracked derived value
d3.p3: effect(() => sideEffect) — auto-reruns when signals inside change, no dependency array
d3.p4: input(), input.required<T>(), output<T>(), model<T>() — signal-based I/O

d4.name: Thursday — Change Detection & OnPush
d4.p1: Zone.js: monkey-patches async APIs → notifies Angular after each completes → full tree CD
d4.p2: Default CD: checks EVERY component after every async event
d4.p3: OnPush — 4 triggers: @Input() reference change, event from component, async pipe, manual markForCheck()
d4.p5: Zoneless: provideZonelessChangeDetection() — no Zone.js, only signals/markForCheck drive CD

d5.name: Friday — Coding Drill
d5.p1: ProductService (DI, providedIn: root, signal for products list)
d5.p2: ProductListComponent (standalone, OnPush): inject service, @for track, computed filter, @if/@empty, @defer
d5.p4: No any types, full TypeScript generics

w2.title: Routing + RxJS + HttpClient
w2.theme: The async layer. RxJS is the hardest React→Angular shift

d1.name: Monday — Router & Lazy Loading
d1.p2: loadComponent and loadChildren for lazy loading
d1.p3: withComponentInputBinding() — route params bind to input() signals automatically
d1.p5: ResolveFn<T> — pre-fetch data before component loads

d2.name: Tuesday — RxJS Fundamentals
d2.p1: Observable<T> — cold by default (new producer per subscriber)
d2.p3: Cold vs hot: HTTP = cold (2 subscribes = 2 requests); Subject = hot
d2.p5: async pipe — subscribes, returns latest value, auto-unsubscribes, triggers OnPush CD

d3.name: Wednesday — RxJS Operators Deep Dive
d3.p1: switchMap — cancels previous inner; use for search/autocomplete
d3.p2: mergeMap — runs concurrently; use for parallel HTTP requests
d3.p3: concatMap — queues in order; use for sequential saves
d3.p4: exhaustMap — ignores new while inner runs; use for login/submit buttons
d3.p5: combineLatest, forkJoin, catchError, debounceTime, distinctUntilChanged, shareReplay(1)

d4.name: Thursday — HttpClient & Interceptors
d4.p2: http.get<T>(url, { params, headers }) — always type the generic
d4.p3: HttpRequest is immutable — always req.clone({ setHeaders: {...} })
d4.p4: HttpInterceptorFn = (req, next) => next(clonedReq) — inject() works inside
d4.p5: withFetch() required for SSR (XHR doesn't work in Node.js)

d5.name: Friday — Timed Challenge
d5.p1: Movie search: HttpClient + debounceTime(300) + distinctUntilChanged + filter + switchMap
d5.p2: isLoading signal (true in tap, false in finalize), errorMessage signal (catchError)
d5.p3: toSignal() or async pipe — not both; OnPush everywhere; track in @for

w3.title: Forms + Pipes + Directives
w3.theme: The declarative layer. Forms are a major exam topic

d1.name: Monday — Reactive Forms & Typed FormControl
d1.p4: Cross-field validator on FormGroup — e.g. password match
d1.p5: AsyncValidatorFn: (ctrl) => Observable<ValidationErrors | null> — debounce with timer()
d1.p6: Always form.getRawValue() on submit — never form.value

d2.name: Tuesday — Template-Driven Forms & ngModel
d2.p1: Import FormsModule in standalone component's imports array — easy to forget
d2.p3: name='fieldName' attribute — REQUIRED for ngForm to track the control
d2.p5: When to use: template-driven for simple forms; reactive for complex validation + testing

d3.name: Wednesday — Pipes
d3.p1: Built-in: currency, date, number, percent, uppercase, lowercase, titlecase, json, async, slice, keyvalue
d3.p2: @if (obs$ | async; as val) — alias avoids double subscription
d3.p3: Pure pipe (default): re-runs only on input reference change — like useMemo
d3.p4: Impure pipe (pure: false): re-runs every CD cycle — expensive; prefer computed() signals
d3.p5: @Pipe({ name: 'x', standalone: true, pure: true }) + implements PipeTransform

d4.name: Thursday — Custom Directives
d4.p5: vcr.createEmbeddedView(tmpl) to render, vcr.clear() to remove

d5.name: Friday — Timed Challenge
d5.p1: Multi-step registration form: firstName, lastName, email (async unique), password, confirmPassword
d5.p2: Cross-field password match validator on FormGroup
d5.p4: appFocusHighlight attribute directive — CSS class on focus/blur via Renderer2

w4.title: Architecture + Performance + SSR
w4.theme: Senior-level thinking. Not just "does it work" but "is it well-designed"

d1.name: Monday — Large-Scale App Architecture
d1.p1: Feature-based folder structure: core/ shared/ features/[feature]/
d1.p2: Smart component: injects facade, OnPush, manages page state
d1.p3: Dumb component: input() / output() only, OnPush, no service injection, easily testable
d1.p4: Facade pattern: wraps store + service; components ONLY talk to facade; exposes read-only signals
d1.p5: APP_INITIALIZER — blocks app startup; ENVIRONMENT_INITIALIZER — feature-level bootstrap

d2.name: Tuesday — State Management
d2.p1: Signals service store: private _x = signal<T>(init) + readonly x = this._x.asReadonly()
d2.p4: When to use: signals service (small team, simple); NgRx (large team, DevTools needed)

d3.name: Wednesday — SSR, SSG & Hydration
d3.p5: TransferState + makeStateKey<T>('key') — avoid double HTTP fetch server→browser

d4.name: Thursday — Performance Optimisation
d4.p1: @defer triggers: on idle|viewport|interaction|hover|timer(ms), when condition, prefetch on idle
d4.p2: @for (x of arr(); track x.id) — always track; $index, $first, $last, $even, $odd
d4.p4: Pure pipe > method in template — methods run every CD cycle, pipes are memoised
d4.p5: NgOptimizedImage: [ngSrc], width, height (required), priority for LCP image

d5.name: Friday — Architecture Challenge
d5.p1: Full e-commerce feature: folder structure, signals store, facade, smart/dumb split
d5.p2: Lazy route, guard, resolver, @defer, SSR guards, NgOptimizedImage, pure pipe

w5.title: Testing + Mock Exams + Weak-Spot Repair
w5.theme: Consolidate, find gaps, simulate the real exam

d1.name: Monday — Testing with TestBed
d1.p2: fixture.detectChanges() — mandatory after every state change (TestBed disables auto-CD)
d1.p3: fixture.componentRef.setInput('propName', value) — correct way to set signal inputs in tests
d1.p4: fakeAsync(() => { tick(300); }) — control time for debounce/interval tests

d2.name: Tuesday — Full Senior Mock Exam
d2.p1: 40 MCQ covering all topics from Weeks 1–4
d2.p2: Answer all 40 before pasting for scoring
d2.p3: Score by category → identify top 3 weak areas → those drive Wednesday

d3.name: Wednesday — Weak-Spot Repair
d3.p1: Per topic: re-explain with fresh angle → 5 targeted questions → 3-in-a-row rule → micro coding exercise
d3.p2: Re-test at end of day — confirm gaps are closed

d4.name: Thursday — Full Coding Simulation
d4.p1: Real exam format: spec given, build from scratch, no lookups
d4.p2: Submit for examiner review with 5 specific questions

d5.name: Friday — Final Review & Exam Day
d5.p1: Morning: 20-concept rapid-fire + 10 React-developer traps + 5 warm-up questions
d5.p2: Exam: certificates.dev/angular — 40 MCQ (90 min) + coding challenge (105 min)

focusTopics.heading: Topics Requiring Extra Time
focusTopics.t1: Dependency Injection — inject(), provider recipes, scoped instances
focusTopics.t2: RxJS operators — especially switchMap vs mergeMap vs exhaustMap
focusTopics.t5: Template-driven forms — ngModel, ngForm, two-way binding
focusTopics.t7: Testing OnPush components — setInput(), detectChanges() timing

tip1.title: Upload the cheat sheets
tip1.description: Download the free Angular Signals and Modern Template Syntax cheat sheets from certificates.dev/cheat-sheets and upload them to your project. Claude can reference them when quizzing you.

tip2.title: Use the exact opening phrase
tip2.description: The session format phrases ('teach me', 'quiz me', 'drill me') are in your project instructions. Claude switches modes based on them — use them consistently.

tip3.title: Paste code as text, not images
tip3.description: When submitting coding challenges, paste raw TypeScript — not screenshots. Claude can read, run, and critique actual code far better than images.

tip4.title: Ask for analogies explicitly
tip4.description: If a React analogy isn't clear, say: 'Give me a more concrete React comparison for this.' Claude will try different angles until it clicks.

tip5.title: Mock exams in one message
tip5.description: For quiz sessions: let Claude give all 10 questions first, then paste all 10 answers at once. Don't answer one at a time — it breaks the exam flow.

tip6.title: The 3-in-a-row rule
tip6.description: In weak-spot sessions, tell Claude: 'Keep drilling me until I get 3 in a row correct, then stop.' This prevents moving on before you're solid.

tip7.title: Before exam day
tip7.description: Ask Claude: 'Give me the 10 most common mistakes React developers make in the Angular senior exam.' This is a high-value 30-minute session.

instructions.heading: Keeping Your Project Instructions Fresh
instructions.desc: The project instructions have a Current Progress section at the bottom. Update it at the start or end of each week — this is how Claude tracks where you are without you explaining it every session.

weekDetail.studySolution: Study the reference solution below. Try the challenge first!

cheatsheet.title: 🐫 Angular
cheatsheet.subtitle: From Angular 16 / 17 → Angular 21. A clear, structured reference for beginners covering evolution, signals, templates, routing, and migration strategy.
cheatsheet.finalDesc: Angular is becoming simpler and more reactive. You don't need to learn everything at once. Start with components, inputs/outputs, and basic signals.

reactAngular.title: React → Angular
reactAngular.subtitle: A mental translation layer for React developers learning Angular. Every concept you already know has a direct Angular equivalent.

coursera.subtitle: Tracking external courses alongside the Angular learning path. This page will be removed once done.

w1d1.sec1Body: In React a function is the component. In Angular a component is a TypeScript class decorated with @Component, always with three distinct parts: class (logic), template (HTML), and an optional stylesheet. The decorator wires them together.
w1d1.sec1CodeCaption: Minimal standalone component — the anatomy at a glance
w1d1.sec3CodeCaption: All three control-flow blocks in one template
w1d1.sec4CodeCaption: input() and output() — the modern, signal-native API
w1d1.sec5Item1: track is MANDATORY in @for — like key in React, but the compiler enforces it
w1d1.sec5Item2: standalone: true means no NgModule — import dependencies directly in @Component
w1d1.sec5Item3: Read an input signal with title() — calling it like a function, not a property
w1d1.sec5Item4: @defer triggers: on viewport | on idle | on interaction | on hover | when condition
w1d1.sec5Item5: Two-way binding [(ngModel)] requires FormsModule in the imports array
w1d1.sec6Q1: What happens if you forget track in @for?
w1d1.sec6A1: Angular will warn in dev mode and fall back to identity tracking — every item re-renders on change, killing performance. Always track by a unique ID.
w1d1.sec6Q2: Can you use @defer on any component, or only standalone ones?
w1d1.sec6A2: In practice @defer works best with standalone components because their bundle can be code-split cleanly. Non-standalone components inside an NgModule cannot be individually deferred.
w1d1.sec6A3: [class.active] toggles a single class. ngClass with an object map {active: bool, disabled: !bool} handles multiple classes at once. Prefer [class.x] for a single class.
w1d1.sec7Item1: Convert this React JSX to an Angular standalone component template: {isLoading ? <Spinner/> : items.map(item => <Card key={item.id} data={item}/>)}
w1d1.sec7Item2: Add an @empty block for when the items array is empty
w1d1.sec7Item3: Use @defer (on viewport) to lazy-load the Card component
w1d1.sec7Item4: Paste your solution into Claude for review
